title.en: Framework selection: go/no-go for auto-loan refinancing
title.es: Selección de marco: ir/no ir para refinanciamiento de préstamos de auto

prompt.en: Capital One is debating entering auto-loan refinancing for used cars. What framework would you use to structure the go/no-go decision, and what are the first three questions that framework generates?
prompt.es: Capital One está debatiendo entrar al refinanciamiento de préstamos de auto para carros usados. ¿Qué marco de trabajo usarías para estructurar la decisión de ir/no ir, y cuáles son las primeras tres preguntas que genera ese marco?

must_include: names the Market Entry framework (or equivalent: size × attractiveness × ability-to-win × NPV/return threshold) as the primary tool, with an explicit reason — e.g., 'this is a new-product / new-segment go/no-go, not a diagnostic or positioning question'

generates at least 3 concrete first questions from the framework — they must be specific to auto-loan refinancing, not generic market-entry questions (e.g., 'what is the addressable balance pool of used-car loans currently above market rate?' not just 'how big is the market?')

acknowledges at least one scenario where the Market Entry framework is insufficient or inappropriate — e.g., 'once we decide to enter, 4P and a profit model take over; Market Entry doesn't design the product'
names which of the three questions is the most critical gate — identifies the single question whose answer would most likely flip the decision
first-level structure is MECE: the questions together probe distinct dimensions of the decision (market attractiveness, competitive position, financial viability) without redundancy
must_avoid: listing multiple frameworks without committing — e.g., 'we could use Market Entry, Profit Tree, or 3C'
applying the Profit Tree as the primary framework — it evaluates unit economics inside an entry decision but does not answer whether to enter
generating generic questions that don't require knowledge of the auto-loan refinancing context — e.g., 'what is our strategy?' or 'what do customers want?'
skipping the ability-to-win dimension — whether Capital One can compete (existing auto-loan infrastructure, data on used-car LTV, dealer relationships) is a critical gate
treating all three questions as equally weighted without identifying a priority gate
